parsed.summary: The v0.4.0→v0.4.6 cumulative diff introduces significant new surface area including retry logic, BigModel error dispatch, job tracking, and state management. While test coverage is extensive, several material risks remain: the retry library's correctness under concurrent/partitioned conditions, state file corruption paths during partial writes, and the tracked-jobs lifecycle potentially leaking jobs under crash scenarios. I could not fully inspect the actual source of every module from the summary alone, but the architecture invites specific failure modes worth blocking on.

findings.title: retry.mjs — retry loop may silently swallow fatal errors or double-execute on stale state
findings.body: The retry module (203 lines added) is the backbone of v0.4.6's auto-retry/backoff for BigModel errors. Without seeing the full implementation, the commit message 'feat: v0.4.6 BigModel error dispatch + auto retry/backoff' implies retries are driven by error classification from bigmodel-errors.mjs. The risk: if error classification returns a false 'retryable' verdict for a non-idempotent operation (e.g., a job submission that already succeeded server-side but timed out on response), the retry will produce duplicate work. Additionally, if retry state is held in-memory only, a process crash mid-retry silently abandons the operation with no recovery path — the user sees neither success nor failure.
findings.recommendation: Verify that: (1) every retried operation is idempotent or carries an idempotency key, (2) retry exhaustion produces a durable error marker visible to the user, (3) the retry loop has a hard ceiling on total wall-clock time (not just attempt count) to prevent indefinite blocking. Add a test for the crash-mid-retry scenario.

findings.title: state.mjs — partial write during crash can corrupt job state file
findings.body: state.mjs (32 lines changed) manages persistent state. If the process is killed (OOM, SIGKILL, power loss) during a write, the state file can be left truncated or containing partial JSON. Subsequent reads will throw a parse error, potentially bricking the user's session with no recovery path. The fs.mjs changes (44 lines) may or may not use atomic write patterns (write-to-temp-then-rename).
findings.recommendation: Ensure all state writes use atomic rename (write to .tmp file, fs.rename to final path). On read, if parse fails, either restore from a .bak of the previous good state or gracefully reset with a warning — do not throw an unrecoverable error.

findings.title: tracked-jobs.mjs — job lifecycle gap: jobs stuck in 'running' after crash with no TTL expiry
findings.body: tracked-jobs.mjs (78 lines changed) manages job tracking for the background/wait execution modes introduced in v0.4.5. If the companion process crashes while jobs are running, those jobs remain in 'running' state forever. On restart, the user would see jobs as 'in progress' that are actually dead. There is no evidence of a TTL, heartbeat, or stale-job detection mechanism in the diff stat.
findings.recommendation: Add a stale-job detection mechanism: either a TTL field set at job creation time (checked on load), or a heartbeat that updates a timestamp periodically. On startup, mark any job that has been 'running' longer than a threshold as 'crashed' or 'unknown'.

findings.title: bigmodel-errors.mjs — error classification is a single point of failure for retry correctness
findings.body: bigmodel-errors.mjs (168 lines) classifies BigModel API errors to decide retry vs fail. If a new error variant is returned by the API (version skew), the default classification determines whether the system retries forever, fails immediately, or silently proceeds. The 189-line test file suggests good coverage of known errors, but the default/fallback case for unrecognized errors is the critical path that determines behavior under future API changes.
findings.recommendation: Ensure the default/fallback error classification is conservative: fail immediately (do not retry) on unrecognized errors, and log the unrecognized error structure so it can be classified in a future release. Add a test for 'unknown error code' that asserts fail-fast behavior.

findings.title: glm-client.mjs — 155 lines changed with no clear timeout/deadline architecture visible
findings.body: glm-client.mjs had 155 lines changed. Combined with retry.mjs, this is the HTTP call path. If the client uses default fetch timeouts (which can be 300s+ in Node.js), a degraded BigModel endpoint combined with aggressive retries could pin the event loop or accumulate hundreds of seconds of wall-clock time per operation. The user sees a hung process.
findings.recommendation: Verify that every outbound HTTP call has an explicit, reasonable timeout (e.g., 30-60s per attempt) via AbortController. Ensure the retry loop's total elapsed time is bounded regardless of per-attempt timeout. Document the effective maximum wall-clock time a user can expect.

findings.title: process.mjs deleted (67 lines) — risk of removed exports still referenced at runtime
findings.body: scripts/lib/process.mjs was entirely removed (67 lines deleted). If any other module dynamically imports or conditionally references exports from process.mjs (e.g., via string interpolation in import paths), this would only fail at runtime under specific code paths — not at module load time.
findings.recommendation: Grep the entire codebase for any remaining reference to 'process.mjs' or its exported symbols. The check-imports.mjs script (5 lines changed) may catch static imports but would miss dynamic imports.

next_steps: Inspect retry.mjs for idempotency guarantees and crash-mid-retry behavior — this is the highest-risk new module.
Inspect fs.mjs and state.mjs for atomic write patterns (write-then-rename vs direct overwrite).
Inspect tracked-jobs.mjs for stale-job TTL or heartbeat mechanism.
Inspect bigmodel-errors.mjs default/fallback error classification path.
Run grep -r 'process\.mjs' across the codebase to confirm no dangling references to the deleted module.
If all five inspections pass cleanly, downgrade to approve.
